one_line_takeaway: An ICLR 2026 workshop SoK paper that reframes agentic-LLM security as a dynamic, cyclic runtime supply-chain problem -- introducing the Man-in-the-Environment adversary, a Data/Tool supply-chain attack taxonomy, and the formalized 'Viral Agent Loop' self-propagation model -- and argues (without building or evaluating one) for a Zero-Trust runtime architecture combining cryptographic tool-capability binding, neuro-symbolic taint tracking, and an isolated auditor-worker semantic firewall.
key_finding: The paper's argued finding (not an empirical result) is that existing localized defenses -- Instruction Hierarchy/Intent Verification at the perception layer, statistical anomaly filtering and audited writes at the memory layer, static registry allowlists and SBOMs at tool resolution/implementation, and 'AI-guarding-AI' semantic firewalls (e.g. Vigil, MCP-Guard) at invocation -- are each structurally insufficient because they inherit assumptions from the static, acyclic software supply-chain model. Specifically the authors argue: (a) structural delimiters against prompt injection can be bypassed by purely semantic payloads because agents continuously upcast passive data into active context (Prompt-Data Isomorphism); (b) statistical memory-poisoning filters fail against structural/graph-based poisoning that appears statistically benign; (c) cryptographic tool-identity verification (allowlists, SBOMs) is blind to an agent's semantic intent, so a MitE-manipulated agent can misuse a perfectly legitimate, verified tool; (d) semantic-firewall/arbiter defenses inherit the same prompt-injection vulnerability because the arbiter model must process the same tainted context. All of the quantitative attack-success figures the paper cites (e.g., a 98% output-steering success rate for multimodal indirect injection, 70% ASR from poisoning 0.1% of a RAG corpus, >80% ASR for AGENTPOISON-triggered backdoors, 76.8% average ASR for MINJA memory injection) are results reported in the respective cited papers, not experiments run by this paper's authors.
core_contribution: This is a Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) paper (published at an ICLR 2026 workshop), not an empirical study. The paper's own contribution is a unified conceptual taxonomy of runtime security risks in agentic LLM systems, organized around a novel framing: agents assemble their execution context and capabilities dynamically at inference time ('Stochastic Dependency Resolution') rather than at build time, which the authors argue makes traditional software-supply-chain security models inapplicable. The paper's original constructs are: (1) the Man-in-the-Environment (MitE) adversary model, distinguished from Man-in-the-Middle by exploiting the agent's epistemic trust in retrieved content rather than compromising a communication channel; (2) 'Prompt-Data Isomorphism', the idea that passive retrieved data is upcast into active instructions during inference; (3) a two-branch taxonomy of the Data Supply Chain (within-session/Contextual-Window manipulation such as indirect prompt injection and many-shot in-context jailbreaking, vs. across-session/External-Memory manipulation such as RAG knowledge-base contamination and long-term memory poisoning); (4) a three-phase taxonomy of the Tool Supply Chain (Phase I Discovery -- intent-to-Tool_ID resolution attacks such as hallucination squatting and semantic masquerading; Phase II Implementation -- Load(Tool_ID)->Runtime Code attacks such as hidden backdoors and transitive dependency exploitation; Phase III Invocation -- Invoke(T,theta,C) attacks such as over-privileged invocation and argument injection); (5) the 'Viral Agent Loop', a formalized model (Eq. 5) of self-propagating agentic worms where one agent's tool side-effect is later retrieved as another agent's input, closing a cycle that traditional (acyclic) supply-chain models cannot represent; and (6) a proposed 'Zero-Trust Agentic Runtime Architecture' with three imperatives: Deterministic Capability Binding (cryptographically bound tool registries instead of semantic/probabilistic tool resolution), Neuro-Symbolic Information Flow Control (runtime taint-tracking of untrusted inputs through the LLM's reasoning chain, backed by cryptographic provenance ledgers), and an Auditor-Worker Architecture (a structurally separate, isolated supervisor model acting as an inline 'semantic firewall' that reviews proposed tool calls before commitment). The paper presents this as forward-looking architecture and future-research directions, not as something it built or evaluated.
threat_model: The Man-in-the-Environment (MitE) adversary does not compromise model weights, system prompts, or hosting infrastructure. Instead it acts as a 'runtime supplier' that injects malicious artifacts into public data sources (wikis, repositories, forums) or tool registries; these artifacts are dormant until voluntarily retrieved/invoked by the agent, at which point they hijack the agent's reasoning loop. Adversary capabilities: (1) inject malicious content into data sources likely to be retrieved, steering reasoning via indirect prompt injection; (2) publish deceptive tools/API wrappers that exploit tool-selection logic. Adversary goals enumerated: stealthy misalignment (deviating from intent without triggering safeguards), confidentiality compromise (information leakage during execution), and propagation/persistence (malicious content re-ingested as future input, i.e. the Viral Agent Loop).
limitations: Explicitly self-acknowledged: none stated as formal 'Limitations' section (the paper instead has Conclusion and Future Directions). Implicit/structural limitations evident from the text: (1) no original empirical validation -- the taxonomy, threat model, and proposed Zero-Trust architecture (Deterministic Capability Binding, Neuro-Symbolic Taint Tracking, Auditor-Worker semantic firewall) are proposed but not implemented or tested by the authors; the Reproducibility Statement's claim of code/datasets in supplementary material is inconsistent with a paper containing no experiments. (2) The authors themselves flag, as future work rather than solved problems, that realistic open-world evaluation benchmarks (beyond closed-world, episodic-amnesia benchmarks like AgentDojo/InjecAgent) do not yet exist to capture persistent memory poisoning and cyclic viral propagation. (3) They acknowledge MCP and similar capability-protocol standardization remain vulnerable to description injection absent 'Name-Squatting Resilient Registries,' which they call necessary but do not design. (4) Neuro-Symbolic Taint Tracking is proposed as a research direction with an acknowledged core difficulty: neural networks can 'wash' provenance/taint tags through paraphrasing, and the paper offers no concrete mechanism resolving this beyond invoking 'Cryptographic Provenance Ledgers' from a separate cited work. (5) The paper discloses use of Gemini to refine text clarity/grammar (LLM Usage Statement), and all authors are listed as independent researchers rather than an institutional lab.
